text: Here you can see the working port incision.
The left lower lobe is severely adherent to the posterior chest wall with signs of pleural fluid and active inflammation.
The ligature is used to separate the adhesions between the left lower lobe and diaphragm as well as the posterior chest wall.
Generally, areas of movement such as the diaphragm and aorta have less adherent planes to dissect the overlying tissues away.
Close to the chronic inflammation, you can see the oozing from all these surfaces that will be controlled later by energy devices.
Here, the plane between the aorta and the lower lobe is being created bluntly.
The OncoMontis bipolar tissue linking device is delivered through the access incision and does a nice job controlling the diffuse oozing typical for these cases.
It is evident that the inflammation extends into the inferior pulmonary ligament.
Both the ligature and the retractor are placed through the working port.
One tool depresses the diaphragm and the other tool divides the inferior pulmonary ligament, extending up to the thickened pleura adjoining the aorta.
This area is also inflamed, so hemostatic agents are placed to promote clotting.
Attention is given to the interlobar fascia to allow dissection to continue without wasting time.
Here a heart port grasper is used to tent the pleura while standard cautery opens the inflamed tissues to expose the pulmonary artery.
Better views of the pulmonary artery result from completely opening fissures.
Here the landing zone for tunneling is created with a peanut dissector at the junction of the interlobar fissure and the medial hilum.
The pulmonary artery is shown there and a straight blunt clamp tunnels beneath the fissure posteriorly to terminate in the landing zone.
This allows passage of a loop to help hold open the tract and then a curb tip stapler hugs the back of this dissection pathway to complete the anterior portion of the interlobar fissure.
Notice how two retractors, both from the anterior working port, provide traction and counter traction.
Once done, it is now useful to complete the posterior fissure.
Again looking posteriorly between the aorta and the hilum, the exit zone is identified and cleaned bluntly.

Then by using a slightly curved blunt clamp through the axis incision and gently spreading in the direction of the landing zone, it is possible to open the posterior fissure to expose the arterial anatomy safely.
Although not always necessary, loops such as this help define the tunnel, particularly when using a standard round tip stapler.
Two retractors from the anterior working ports provide traction and counter traction, and a peanut blunt tip retractor further defines the arterial anatomy.
a blunt instrument gently separates it away from lymphatic tissue.
A surgical stapler or a 5mm energy sealing device can then be passed between the two retractor instruments through the same anterior working hole.
Here you can see the curb tip stapler dividing the remaining posterior fissure to expose the superior segmental artery.
Notice that this artery arises from the continuation pulmonary artery proximal to the lingular artery.
Again, using the same maneuver is divided with a curb tip stapler to preserve the continuation pulmonary artery down to the origin of the lingular artery.
The base of the artery branches are then dissected bluntly from the surrounding inflamed tissue.

Here a large right angle clamp is shown in accelerated speed demonstrating how multiple small spreads with no more force than the weight of the instrument will help it open the tunnel beneath the vessel.
Then using the supplied silicone extension of the curb tip stapler, the anvil is guided through safely to allow division of this artery while preserving flow to the lingular segment.
The curb tip extension does not require removal before firing the stapler.
Pulsatile flow into the lingular artery is verified after the oblique firing of this vascular stapler.
Now the lung is transferred to a ring retractor passed through the axis incision to allow separation of the inflamed thickened tissues.
If there is any question about aberrant venous drainage, vascular staplers can be used to divide these tissues.
This central lower lobe tumor was adherent to the esophagus.
You can see muscle fibers being thinned out and dissected off of the tumor.
This is a useful diamond flex retractor, initially developed for laparoscopic liver retraction.
Even larger specimens like this can be controlled with a single instrument, provided the dissection has been performed to the point that the diamond flex retractor can be passed around the hilum.
This 5mm retractor also allows other instruments, such as a stapler, to pass beside it.
Here you can see the bulky tumor anatomy and its adherence to the esophagus and pericardium that keeps the inferior pulmonary vein within it from being divided at this point.
Lift nodes such as the level 8 station are shown.
To open the pericardium it is grasped at an area where the heart can be seen moving beneath it.
Here you can see pericardial fluid egressing
and with an endoscopic scissors passed through the axis incision, further opening into the pericardium is created to allow safe visualization of the inferior pulmonary vein.
In this case, it would have been very difficult to divide this vascular structure without entering the pericardium.
Further dissection of the pericardium off of the vein circumferentially is performed.
Here a large right angle clamp can be passed safely around the pulmonary vein and its insertion into the left atrium.
This right angle clamp can deliver a catheter to help guide the curb tip stapler through a tight passage such as demonstrated here.
One advantage of the curb tip anvil extension is that it no longer requires using the whole open flange of such a leader catheter.
The tubing can be cut simply.
In this case, it is a 14 French Red Rover catheter.
The catheter can be stretched onto the tip, just like the leader that comes with this product, except that it has the advantage of being much longer.
Since this stapler is going across thicker atrial tissue, notice that we are using the longer length purple load tri-staple cartridge.
In this view, you can see the tumor and its effect on tissues immediately above the stapler.
Now the remaining pericardium and inflamed tissues around the bronchus are divided with an energy device.
The surgeon can help fill for the bronchus within the structure using the suction catheter.
Here you can see a lymph node grasper removing an 11L lymph node.
The same DiamondFlex retractor provides downward force to lengthen the bronchus so that a black tri-staple load cartridge can be positioned and closed.
Green or black load staplers are useful for thick tissue like the bronchus.
Here the DiamondFlex retractor remains in and provides a convenient way to provide base stabilization of the triangular opening into the extraction sac.
Large specimens such as this are manipulated into the extraction sac using cooperating instruments, one through the axis incision and one sharing the anterior working port.
The site of the tumor is inspected and here you can see application of fibrin glue sealant to the area of previous oozing and placement of a chest tube.
